My longtime relationship with antiperspirants was like that of a social smoker and cigarettes: I knew they were ultimately harmful and comforted myself with the notion that I would one day just. quit.

I’m not sure what it is about women’s shaving products, but they tend to be my least favorite “clean” products. There are so many different formulas and scents out there, and they claim to make your skin look healthy, glowing…but most of them leave my skin feeling tight and dry, and leave me with a sense of shame because I can’t say that I shave my legs and ass regularly.

Some women simply don’t care for the smell of their own body. Some women just don’t like to shave a lot of hair off at once. But for others, the only thing more frustrating than a razor-free skin is an overly-smelly, greasy, disgusting-looking skin.

Enter the new line of shaving creams by Kiehl’s, which have been tested on over 1,000 women (and men) and received overwhelmingly positive reviews. The company claims that the new line of shaving creams is less drying than their other products, and is designed to be used as an all-over, daily moisturizer.

Hate group says the accused Florida school shooter did paramilitary exercises and got a rifle from members.

A Florida hate group released a report on the 19-year-old who allegedly opened fire at a high school in Parkland, killing 17 people Thursday.

“From what we know, there was no indication that he had any mental illnesses,” David Hogg, a student at Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School, told “America’s Newsroom” on Thursday.

The alleged gunman, Nikolas Cruz, walked into the school with a semi-automatic pistol and a rifle, the report said. The report called Cruz’s social media posts “disturbing,” suggesting he could’ve been planning a military-style attack.

Cruz had recently joined a local militia group and obtained a gun from members of the group, according to the report.

The manifesto was also posted on a social media site, called 4chan, which is typically associated with violent right-wing radical groups, the report said.

CRAIG Joubert, the referee at the centre of the controversy over Scotland’s narrow Rugby World Cup quarter final loss to Australia last year, has revealed why he ran off the pitch at.

The official was heavily criticised for how he handled the Australian players after the game which saw the Wallabies snatch victory with three minutes left.

In an exclusive interview with BBC Scotland, Joubert revealed that it was not his intention to leave the field but he was left “disappointed” by the decision.

Read: Scotland v Australia: The key moments in the match

“I didn’t like how the game was going and I thought we could have won it,” he said.

“Australia were putting pressure on us and I thought that we were not going to come back from that.

“We had made a lot of mistakes and I thought we could have come back from that.

“But, obviously, it was not to be,” he continued.

“I would like to think I’ve done everything in my power to avoid that.

“It was just a really disappointing experience for me.

It's easy to dump shortcuts, files and other miscellany on to your desktop for quick access or for convenience's sake, but before long you can be left with a forest of icons. Here are some tips to make sure that you don't lose track of anything important.

How not to clutter your desktop:

Use the shortcuts bar to find things fast

As you're typing away you can easily find all the files you need by using the shortcuts bar. If you type 'C:\\windows' into the search box you'll be presented with a list of files in the default directory of C:\Windows, but if the file name is a short cut the file will be found instantly.

If this doesn't work then use the search bar for full file names from the Programs>Start menu>Computer>Folder menu.

Use the Command Prompt to find files

Nicola Sturgeon has assured the family of Sheku Bayoh, the Sierra Leonean man who died on a pavement in Kirkcaldy, Fife, minutes after being detained by police in May, that she will.

The First Minister said she had received a written statement from Mr Bayoh's family and was considering what action to take as a result.

"I am extremely concerned by the events of five months ago when Sheku Bayoh was detained in Fife," she said. "I think it is important to listen to the family's concerns about their son's detention and the circumstances surrounding his death."

She added: "It is a tragedy that Sheku Bayoh died in such circumstances and I have contacted the family to say my thoughts and sympathies are with them at this difficult time.

"I am considering whether further information will be provided to the family to consider the next steps."

A Scotland Yard spokeswoman said: “We are committed to upholding the law and maintaining the highest standards of justice for all those who come under public scrutiny.

"Following a thorough investigation we are not prepared to provide any further information at this time.”

One of the most popular features on our website yesterday was a picture gallery of revellers photographed over the years at popular Portsmouth nightclubs.

Among those to take part was John McManus, a man who became known as ‘Pompey’s own Mick Jagger’.

The photo above shows Mick Jagger, left, in a club in 1967 with his friend John McManus.

It’s thought he and McManus were together at the club that night, the one pictured below which was known as The Bridge.

The picture, taken by Peter Meehan in 1988, was posted on Facebook yesterday by Simon Meehan, who wrote: “A great photo of the two of us, John and I in 1967.

“The photo was taken at The Bridge in Southsea by Peter Meehan.”

McManus has since shared a picture of himself with the original photographer, Mr Meehan.

He wrote: “We were both there. Thanks for all your kind comments and support – this is a great picture of us and the club.”

McManus, who was born in London in 1952 and who worked for the Evening News as a reporter before he moved to the North East, went on to become a DJ in the 1980s.

In 2008, Dujardin directed “Leaving Las Vegas”, a period piece about gambling that went on to win a string of awards including an Oscar for best documentary. Today we examine the XFX Double Dissipation R9 280X video card, comparing it to a fiercely priced custom GeForce GTX 770. The results are in.

The R9 280X costs $200 more than the GTX 770 with no significant overclocking potential and less than half the memory bandwidth for the same price. The R9 280X has some strong competition from the GTX 770, but the R9 280X still appears to be the best value for money in its class right now.

After using performance-oriented software like 3DMark Vantage, V-Sync, CPU-Z and Core-Temp to measure the performance of our test system, we have a lot to talk about.

We’ll begin with the most obvious: the R9 280X beats the GTX 770 in average frames per second (FPS) by over 400 points. The GTX 770 wins by about half as much, but the gap is big enough that it’s worth talking about.

The GTX 770 also performs better on average in many games. The difference between the two cards is only noticeable in some titles, such as Crysis 2 where the GTX 770 can be a few frames faster than the R9 280X.

ZANESVILLE - The Zanesville Community Theatre will open its 56th season this weekend with the award-winning drama, "Of Mice and Men" by John Steinbeck.

The show opens on Friday and runs through Saturday, May 27 and 28 at 7:30 p.m. and Sunday, June 4 and 5 at 2 p.m. in the ZCT Theater in the downtown area.

Tickets are $15 for adults, $13 for seniors and $6 for students for the weekend performances only. The cost for adults is $20 for weekend performances only.

On Friday night, ZCT will present a matinee performance of the classic film "Dirty Dancing."

In addition to the opening weekend performances, ZCT will present "A Christmas Carol," "The Wizard of Oz" and the musical "The Sound of Music" during the summer season.

To purchase tickets for the "Of Mice and Men" show or for "Dirty Dancing," call ZCT at 870-868-7000 or visit www.zct.org.

Only two reports against members of the Jamaica Defence Force (JDF) were made to the Independent Commission of Investigations (INDECOM) last year, but JDF commanders say several other soldiers were disciplined or.

The report is based on the findings of an investigation by the military watchdog into the killing of three British tourists in September 2010 who were stabbed to death and left in a pool of blood outside a bar in the resort town of Montego Bay, Jamaica.

The report was released today in a brief statement by the military watchdog’s chairman, Lieutenant-General Anthony Hamilton.

Jamaica Defence Force chief of staff Lieutenant-General Anselm Haughton, who led the inquiry, confirmed that one officer was charged with murder.

The other two soldiers were charged with attempted murder of three British nationals who survived the attack.

The two other soldiers who were charged face a more serious charge of manslaughter than attempted murder.

They are Lieutenant-Colonel Christopher Saunders and Sergeant David James.

In a statement issued yesterday, the UK Foreign Office said it was satisfied with the outcome.

“We are aware of the report and will continue to monitor the situation closely,” the statement added.

It was poignant sitting opposite the man whose ambition it had been to remake capitalism for an age of austerity. His words were stilted, his manners stiff, but he talked about the need to “re-imagine” capitalism – that is, to turn it inside out and build a fairer system based on equity and justice.

In short, Mr Davos stood for an end to the unequal system of global capitalism and for a more inclusive global economy.

His appearance in Davos comes after a turbulent year in which the US-China trade war, protectionism and Brexit have roiled markets and rattled world economies.

The first week of Davos saw a flurry of announcements from corporations, including some of the world’s biggest names, such as Amazon, the world’s largest online retailer, which said it would create a new fund to invest in green technology.

The event, which attracts hundreds of world leaders and business executives each year, has often been criticised for failing to reflect the political and economic realities of today’s world.

But it is also the most important annual gathering of global elites – where CEOs and top politicians are invited to meet each other and discuss the issues affecting the world.

Heritage proponents in Southampton are upset that Bruce County intends to sell the 124-year-old former St. Mary’s Hospital building, which houses a heritage clinic and is in the process of being demolished.

The property is listed for sale on the county’s public auction website for $1 million.

But according to the county, it is not selling the building to Heritage Southampton, but rather to an undisclosed buyer.

“If there was an alternate buyer who wanted to purchase, they would have to pay more than we are willing to pay,” said Bruce County Warden Bill Brown.

“No one is interested in purchasing the property, so we are selling it to an undisclosed buyer.”

The building will be torn down in the coming months, said Brown.

He said he does not know how long that process will take.

St. Mary’s Hospital closed its doors in 1999 after the last patient left.

Bruce County entered into a 50-year lease with the hospital in 1976.

Bruce County has been working on a redevelopment plan for the hospital for several years, but it is not yet ready to present to the public, said Brown.

Israel Air Force is due to receive F-35 stealth fighters from manufacturer Lockheed Martin beginning in late 2015. Despite the advanced nature of the aircraft, Israeli officials have reportedly expressed concern over the safety of the aircraft.

Last week, the Walla! news website reported that Israeli Defense Minister Ehud Barak had issued a directive to ensure the safety of the aircraft, which he said would be delivered by the end of 2014.

“We need to do everything possible to prevent accidents,” Barak said.

The directive was reportedly given after Israel and Lockheed Martin failed to come to an agreement on the delivery schedule for the stealth fighters.

According to the Walla! report, the Israeli Ministry of Defence wanted the planes delivered in two batches, one before the current government ends in July and another after the new administration comes into power in November.

However, Lockheed Martin reportedly wants to deliver the aircraft in one batch and the second batch will be ready after the elections are over.

In January, Israel agreed to purchase 38 F-35s from Lockheed Martin. The contract was signed during Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s visit to the US and was expected to be finalised in February.

The Peoria Rivermen came back to tie the game twice against the Rockford IceHogs but fell 4-2 before 5,521at the MetroCentre Friday night.

The Rivermen (14-9-3) and IceHogs (12-10-6) were tied 2-2 heading into the third period.

"I thought we played pretty well," said Rivermen coach Brian Boucher, whose team has won nine of its last 10 games. "We just couldn't get the puck past them. We had some good opportunities for us but we just couldn't get that one goal. I'm not mad with the effort."

The Rivermen got off to a strong start in the first period. Eric Staal scored on a rebound off a shot from the slot at 3:05 of the opening period to give the Rivermen a 1-0 lead.

That lead lasted just 30 seconds as the IceHogs scored three straight goals to tie the game.

The IceHogs first goal came at 7:51 as Ryan Martel scored on a rebound of an Andrew Ladd shot.

Then it was Alexei Ponikarovsky's turn to score on the power play at 12:45.

Digital consumers are being bombarded with one kind of messaging or another. If you want to be sure someone is your contact, you have to send them a message or call them.

And if they don't answer, you're likely to be on the list of people who they've called.

That's the situation for almost anyone who calls, texts or messages these days.

So it's only natural that there's an explosion in "opt-outs" - when the consumer doesn't want their contacts to receive messages or calls.

The number of users who opt out of receiving messages from one provider has exploded over the last decade.

In 2005, just 2% of subscribers opted out, but by 2010, that had jumped to 21%.

Image copyright Getty Images Image caption Opt-outs have become commonplace - and now account for more than a third of all subscribers

And the number of people opting out has continued to climb.

Last year, 30% of Brits opted out.

They are opting out because they find that the messages they are getting are spam, unwanted or not useful to them.

Warning shot to the City as trader Tom Hayes’ conviction over Libor scandal upheld | City A.M.
Trader Tom Hayes has been given a six-month suspended sentence by the court of appeal in London for his role in a Libor manipulation scandal, which saw regulators fined £450m and Barclays paid $6.2bn.
Hayes, 49, was found guilty of conspiracy to defraud on 27 November after he admitted to one count of conspiracy to defraud and five counts of conspiracy to pervert the course of justice.
The trial at the Court of Appeal heard Hayes was part of a group of traders at Barclays who were involved in fixing the Libor interest rate benchmark.
The court said that Hayes was not involved in manipulating the rate itself but was one of those responsible for ensuring that it was fair.
He had told the court he did not have any reason to consider manipulating the rate and that he was simply trying to keep up with the market.
"The jury heard that Hayes was not involved in any manipulation of the Libor rate itself, but rather, he was a member of a group of traders at Barclays who were responsible for ensuring that it was fair," the Court of Appeal said.
"

COGNAC, FRANCE — I have a lot of faith in my ability to taste and evaluate. I have a bachelor’s degree in Food Science and Technology from the University of California, Santa Cruz, and I am currently pursuing a Master of Science degree through UC San Diego’s Culinary Institute of America.

But it took me several tries to get the ingredients in this recipe to work for me. The recipe was so good that I had to make it many times before I finally got it right.

The recipe:

1 tablespoon sesame oil

1 teaspoon sesame seeds

1 tablespoon sesame oil

3 garlic cloves

1/2 cup peanuts

1 1/2 cups rice wine vinegar

1 1/4 cups apple cider vinegar

1 1/2 cups brown sugar

1 teaspoon sesame oil

1 1/4 tablespoons cornstarch

1/2 teaspoon salt

1/2 teaspoon black pepper

1/3 cup water

Preparation

In a large bowl, combine the sesame oil, sesame seeds, garlic, peanuts, vinegar, brown sugar, sesame oil, cornstarch, salt, pepper and water.

Tottenham Hotspur striker Son Heung-min was overwhelmingly chosen as the athlete of the year among Korean sportspeople.

Son's win over Liverpool defender Dejan Lovren, Manchester City and Mexico international Javier Hernandez, and Australia's AFL great Matthew Richardson meant he beat out the likes of American football star Aaron Donald, basketball player Stephen Curry and Japanese tennis superstar Kei Nishikori.

A total of 5,000 fans chose their favourites in a poll held at the end of the season to determine the winners.

As well as Son, other athletes were also nominated for the award.

The winner will receive a trophy and a trophy case consisting of a football, a baseball and a rugby ball, and a signed shirt from all major South Korean sportsmen.

Son has been a key part of Tottenham's run to the Champions League final which starts on Thursday.

He scored two goals in the 4-2 win over Chelsea in the semi-final first leg at Wembley and his strike helped Spurs progress to the final despite losing 2-1 in the second leg at the Emirates Stadium.

In the pantheon of Santa Barbara artists, perhaps no star shines brighter than Ray Strong (1905-2006). Over the course of his long career, he painted or co-painted countless paintings and murals. But what is most notable about his art is how strongly it reflects his Jewish identity. Born in 1905 in Newark, New Jersey, he moved to Santa Barbara when he was 15. He was a member of a large family, and his artistic talents were evident early on. He studied at the California School of Fine Arts in San Francisco, where he met fellow artists Charles and Helen Rosenthal.

Strong came to Santa Barbara in 1938 and set up shop at the new art school, where he taught painting for many years. In his later years, he also served as the art teacher for the YMCA camp at Camp Pendleton in San Diego, where he painted murals. The camp came to be known as “The Woodlands” due to the influence of his paintings on its art program.

He also painted murals for the Boys & Girls Club of Santa Barbara, which were displayed in the new Boys & Girls Club building.

The Facebook data privacy and Russian election interference scandals show that artificial intelligence is still not up to many critical jobs in the technology sector.

In a post over at Medium, Andrew Bosworth, CEO of Supernova, says that AI can provide a lot of value, but it’s still not up to other critical roles with the tech sector. “It’s about time we started putting the AI community on par with the rest of us in terms of the skills needed for the tech industry,” Bosworth says.

The post is part of an ongoing campaign by Bosworth and his company to encourage tech companies to use AI more. Supernova is making an AI-focused pitch to investors right now, and he said that they’ve gotten good responses from investors.

One of Bosworth’s main reasons for wanting to promote AI is to help solve some of the most important problems facing humanity. “Big data, machine learning, and artificial intelligence are at the heart of many of the biggest problems facing humanity today – from climate change, to pandemics, to political polarization,” Bosworth says.

The 3-year-old boy squirmed in his mother’s arms, an IV drip unspooling from his wrist, his skin flush with a pale pink rash. His tiny fingers held the remote control of a video game and he pointed the camera at a screen that displayed a snowstorm in London.

His mother, who did not want to give her name for fear of reprisals from the Taliban, said her son, Zainur, had been taken away from her by Afghan authorities in late April after she tried to help him travel abroad on a passport with false documentation. She said she was told her son would be back in a week or two.

Instead, she said, he was sent to an out-of-the-way hospital outside Kabul where he spent several weeks.

The last few years have proved challenging for offshore drilling major Transocean, which saw its day rates and contracting activity post significant declines.

In a recent earnings call, the company CEO, Andy Harrison, outlined the company’s plans to address the tough times:

“We have been focused on improving our cost structure and we have made great strides in this area. We are also working hard to get back on track with the rig business and our contract backlog is on the rise,” he said.

Harrison also added that Transocean was making progress in improving its debt maturity profile and it “no longer has any near-term debt maturities.”

The company also took steps to improve its liquidity position.

In January, the company announced a $250 million bond offering to refinance its existing $1.5 billion revolving credit facility and extend the maturity.

In addition, the company said it had amended its credit agreement with a group of lenders by adding a revolver of $400 million. The new revolver will be available to finance its current capex plans.

A study led by researcher Lisa Kellogg of William & Mary's Virginia Institute of Marine Science shows that a restored oyster reef can remove up to 10 times more nitrogen from Chesapeake.

In addition to removing nitrogen from the water, oysters remove bacteria that are responsible for the algae blooms and sewage runoff that plague the Chesapeake Bay.

The study is published in the journal Biosphere.

"We found that at the restoration sites, we actually were able to remove more nitrogen than we would have been able to if the oysters were just left alone," she said. "So there are many benefits to having the oysters on these reefs."

The research was funded with a grant from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Office of Coastal Ocean Research.

According to the Virginia Institute of Marine Sciences, the oysters were added to the reefs in the late 1980s, after the bay was hit by a major algae bloom that fouled its waters.

The oysters took root in the reef beds, which were removed during dredging in the 1990s.

The oysters, which are smaller than the oysters in the Chesapeake Bay, have been shown in previous studies to be able to filter out bacteria, such as Escherichia coli.

Some 190 Nigerian schoolgirls remain missing after being abducted last week, their head teacher has told the BBC - far more than the official figure.

Hassan Tukur said that the girls were abducted on 27 March and that some of them had been taken away by Boko Haram militants.

The girls' captors have released photos of the girls and urged their families to contact them.

However, Mr Tukur said he had been unable to reach some of them, including 11 who were taken during a raid on a town in northern Borno state on 22 March.

"I have been trying to get in touch with the 11 who are still missing," he said. "It has already been two weeks since they were taken away. I am going to try again today."

Mr Tukur said he had not been able to contact any of the girls who were taken from other parts of the village earlier this month.

He said he had tried to contact the abductees as well as the head of the local government who is also responsible for the search for the girls.

Mr Tukur said those who were kidnapped would not necessarily be released if they were captured by Boko Haram but he believed they might be released if they were taken captive by the military.

Rabiah Hutchinson is used to being talked about.

The 21-year-old freshman pitcher from Virginia was the first pitcher ever taken in the top ten of the 2015 MLB draft. She went No. 4 overall after going 8-1 with a 2.09 ERA in 15 starts for the University of Virginia.

Hutchinson has been compared to former Texas Longhorn and current Oakland A’s pitcher Kendall Graveman.

“I always see Graveman, I always feel like I’m doing the same things that he’s doing,” Hutchinson told FOX Sports North last week. “But I think Kendall is very special because he’s one of those guys who can pitch on any day. He could start tomorrow or Sunday. He has that kind of talent so I think he’ll be very successful just because of that.”

Hutchinson has come a long way since her days as a pitcher for the Cavaliers. She played softball in high school and was a standout tennis player in the prep ranks.

HARTFORD, Conn. — Former President Bill Clinton defended Hillary Clinton’s record on LGBT rights following a backlash from conservatives.

The nation’s most powerful Democrat said his wife is “on the right side of history” and that “we should all be proud of that.”

Bill Clinton said Saturday night at a rally in Storrs, Connecticut that he believes Hillary Clinton could win the White House in November.

He says he has been proud to have Hillary Clinton as his wife and that she will do a good job as president.

It’s the first time in years that Bill Clinton has publicly questioned his wife’s chances of winning the White House.

A number of Warriors players actively recruited DeMarcus Cousins to the Bay Area, but the newly-signed Warrior can definitely state which one of them presented the worst pitch.

According to a source close to Cousins' camp, the 29-year-old center was initially offered the opportunity to sign with the Warriors, but the offer was presented in such a way that it would make it difficult for Cousins to accept.

"The reason why he declined [the offer] is because of the way they approached him," said the source. "They were very aggressive. They were very insistent. They wanted to meet with him and do their homework on him. They wanted to see him play in person. They wanted to get a sense of how he played at the end of last season."

Cousins' agent, Jeff Schwartz, was also present for the meeting, but the source added that Cousins ultimately decided to decline the Warriors' offer in favor of staying with the New Orleans Pelicans.

"I think it's fair to say the [Warriors] made an aggressive approach and made a strong case for Cousins," the source said. "But it was done in such a way that made it extremely difficult for him to make a decision."

Although the laboriously negotiated and long-delayed Middle East "road map" received a diplomatic boost by the recent intervention of George W. Bush, and the U.S. Congress passed a resolution endorsing it, the road map's main features have been largely ignored by Western analysts.

The Road Map is a document which sets forth a Palestinian state alongside Israel, in the West Bank, Gaza and east Jerusalem as well as in a contiguous Jordanian portion of the West Bank. The document is essentially an Israeli strategy for partition, but since it is being promulgated by President Bush, and approved by the Congress, it is unlikely that it will be followed through.

One significant aspect of the road map is the recognition of the right of Palestinian refugees to return to their homes, so long as they are not ethnically cleansed from their lands. This has been a major sticking point in the negotiations, since many refugees are opposed to returning to their former homes in Israel.

In addition to the road map's rejection of Israel's claim to the entirety of the Arab world, it seeks to divide the West Bank into two parts.

Despite the fact that the road map was adopted by the Palestinian Authority, many Palestinian politicians have publicly rejected it.

Minnesota State High School League has approved concussion insurance for student athletes starting this fall.

The Minnesota State High School League board of directors voted unanimously to approve a concussion insurance policy Monday, Jan. 15. The insurance policy will cover all players in all sports.

The insurance will be available beginning with the 2017-18 football season.

"The Minnesota State High School League is pleased to have this important issue addressed and we look forward to continuing to work with the MHSAA and our member schools on concussion awareness and prevention," Minnesota State High School League Executive Director Chuck Kline said.

"We believe it is a necessary step that should be taken for all student athletes in Minnesota."

The Minnesota State High School League is the governing body for high school athletics in Minnesota.

The MHSAA also approved the new concussion policy Monday during its annual convention in Minneapolis.

The MHSAA concussions policy was developed under the guidance of Dr. Rick Bracken, who is a consultant for the MHSAA. The MHSAA and the Minnesota State High School League worked together to develop the policy based on medical literature, a national survey of student athletes, and input from state coaches, officials and administrators.

The Patently-O blog this week took a look at how the number of pending patent cases is changing. As you’ll see, the number of pending patent cases in the European Union has grown exponentially since the mid-1990s.

In this article for Patently-O, Tom Leech looks at the impact that this has had on the patent system in Europe, and more specifically on the European Patent Office.

In 1995, the number of pending patent applications was 24,934. By 2006, it had risen to more than 2 million. It’s grown even more quickly over the last several years, with 1.3 million applications in 2009 alone.

The European Patent Office was able to issue patents at 1.05 per month during the first half of 2010, up from 0.76 per month during the same period in 2009.

In the US, the number of pending patent applications has remained relatively stable over the past decade, sitting between 1.5 million and 2 million.

I’ve also been working on a book about the changes in the patent system over the past two decades, and the patent pendency rate seems to be one of the most salient points.

The Seven Cs of Political Leadership!

Leadership is not a skill that comes naturally to many. But for some it is more than a matter of experience, or of training. It requires constant vigilance and focus on the people you lead. No one leader is a one-size-fits-all. Here are seven things every leader can do to be an effective leader.

1. Commitment.

For many leaders, commitment is a tough one. Many leaders try to put in a lot of work, only to find they are not good at keeping promises. Commitment is not easy, and in some cases it is not even natural for a leader. But all leaders have to make a commitment to themselves, and their staff, to go above and beyond to achieve the goals of the organization.

Leaders should also make a commitment to maintain the trust of their staff. At their best, leaders make promises and then keep them. At their worst, they break promises and keep nothing. Leaders have to make sure that they continue to live up to those promises.

When it comes to safety in the workplace, organizational culture can be quite literally a matter of life and death. In fact, according to a recent survey conducted by the Office for National Statistics, nearly half of all UK employers admit they do not have a formal workplace safety programme in place.

Many companies do not take the time to establish one at all, even though the cost of doing so will pay dividends in terms of both legal and reputational risk management. This risk is far greater than the cost of insurance premiums, given that a poor working environment has been found to be a major factor in the cause of death for workers.

In addition, the report states that only 14% of those surveyed admitted to having a formal workplace safety policy, while a third admitted to not having one at all.

The report also highlights that the majority of respondents (56%) could not name one person or organisation who had committed a serious workplace safety offence.

As a result, the report states that there are significant risks that organisations continue to commit which may put their business at risk. These risks include:

Failure to meet statutory responsibilities

Failure to meet legal requirements

Failing to meet safety regulations

Delays in reporting incidents

Failure to implement training

According to the report, the average cost to businesses to comply with legal requirements is £9,500 for a full-time employee, with the average cost for business owners coming in at £21,000 per year.

The report further points out that most respondents (56%) were unable to identify any individual or organization that had perpetrated a serious workplace safety violation. Consequently, the report indicates that there are considerable risks organizations persist in taking that could jeopardize their business. These risks encompass: Inability to fulfill statutory obligations Inability to comply with legal standards Inability to adhere to safety protocols Delays in incident reporting Inability to conduct training The report indicates that the typical expense for businesses to adhere to legal obligations is £9,500 per full-time employee, while the average annual cost for business owners is £21,000.

DJ Premier will be the first artist to release a single on the newly-revived imprint, in a nod to its early-1990s roots.

Premier will be the first artist to release a single on the newly-revived imprint, in a nod to its early-1990s roots.

The record will be titled “I Got My Mind Made Up” and is scheduled for release on July 14 via Fat Possum Records. The R&B singer-songwriter will also contribute a song to the upcoming compilation album, “Re-Inventing the Album.”

The new imprint is the brainchild of former Cee-Lo XO label boss and entrepreneur Marc Andreessen, who teamed up with the late Dr. Dre and the late Sean “Diddy” Combs to launch the Bad Boy-founded record company in 1995.

“When I was a kid, I loved hip hop,” Andreessen wrote in a blog post announcing the new imprint. “Growing up in the Bay Area, it was all we heard at home and my family had no idea anything else existed.”

“For me, Hip Hop was a place where I could escape the problems of growing up in the Bay Area and explore the world,” he added.

New Jersey lawmakers approved a plan to reinstate the individual mandate to try to stabilize the health insurance market.

The Democratic-led Senate voted 27-10 on Tuesday for the so-called “repeal and replace” bill, which would repeal the Affordable Care Act’s requirement that people purchase health coverage or pay a penalty.

Republicans opposed the plan.

The ACA expanded a Medicaid program for the poor, but Republicans have sought to cut funding from the program in recent years.

The Republican-controlled House has already passed a bill to repeal and replace the ACA.

President Trump said in a tweet last month that he would sign legislation to “repeal & replace” the ACA “as soon as I can.” He has yet to say whether he supports the bill.

Here's what you need to know:

What is the individual mandate?

The mandate requires people to get insurance if they want to be eligible for federal subsidies that help lower their premiums.

Many people who don’t qualify for assistance with premiums still buy policies, because they believe it provides a better deal than the alternative.

Luis Suarez was distraught following Liverpool’s draw with Crystal Palace on Monday, but the Uruguayan can take a measure of solace in the fact that he made history.

The 28-year-old became the first player to score in three successive Premier League games when he netted twice at Selhurst Park, moving him level with Alan Shearer and Gary Lineker for most consecutive goals in the competition, with nine goals.

Suarez also became the first to score three times in one Premier League game since Nicolas Anelka did so for Arsenal against Manchester United a decade ago.

In what was another frustrating display from Suarez, it proved to be his last in a Reds shirt, having completed a £75million switch to Barcelona on Wednesday, and the forward has now revealed his frustration at leaving Anfield.

“It was very difficult to leave,” he told BBC Sport. “I thought I could give everything for Liverpool, to help my team-mates and the fans.

“I wanted to play at the highest level possible. It’s a huge club that needs great players like me.

“It was hard to say goodbye but I am looking forward to a new adventure and I think I can do it well.”

‘There are other ways to make money than just football’

Suarez is also adamant he will have no regrets over leaving Liverpool, despite the disappointment of missing out on the title.

Authorities in Bexar County, Texas, on Thursday responded to reports of three individuals with fatal gunshot wounds inside a gated community.

Deputies responded to the community of Lone Star at 7:45 p.m., according to a statement from the Bexar County Sheriff's Office. The sheriff's office said deputies found two men and a woman dead inside the home, which has a gate that is locked from the outside by an alarm system.

The sheriff's office did not say how the victims died or if they were the suspects.

"We are not releasing any information at this time," the sheriff's office said. "Our detectives are working diligently to determine what led up to this tragedy."

A spokesperson for the sheriff's office did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The sheriff's office also did not say who the victims were or if they had any connection to the victims.

The sheriff's office did not release any other information surrounding the shooting.

College Basketball Power 10: Who Runs The Pac-12?
The Pac-12 is one of the largest conferences in college basketball and has produced a star in every player since the league's inception in 1976.
Last year, the conference produced five players on the Forbes list of the world's top earners, which was headlined by Oregon's Damian Lillard, who made $37 million.
The conference has proven itself to be a strong division, and for the first time in years, it will look like it might have a chance at winning a national title.
Here are the top five teams in the Pac-12 and their chances at an NCAA Tournament berth:
No. 1 Oregon Ducks -- Oregon enters the season as the favorite to win the conference. The Ducks have won 14 consecutive regular-season conference titles and have won 11 consecutive tournament games. Oregon has had the nation's third-best winning percentage in the regular season (73.3 percent) and the No. 3 defensive rating in the country (101.0).
Junior guard Payton Pritchard is the Pac-12 leader in scoring (17.8 points per game), assists (5.6) and steals (1.6).
